Project · 2025 · Sponsored by Salt River Project

Dual-Axis
Solar Tracker

A photovoltaic alignment system that keeps a solar panel perpendicular to the sun throughout the day. Arduino UNO R3, four photoresistors in cross configuration, two servo motors, closed-loop feedback at 60 ms intervals.

§ 01 / ABSTRACT

Engineering vision.

The project brief in the team's own words. The Dual Axis Solar Tracker represents the fusion of innovation, sustainability, and teamwork — developed under ENGR-102B, sponsored by Salt River Project.

“
Great engineering isn't built alone — it is a product of collaboration, curiosity, and collective genius.
Team motto · The Martians

The Dual Axis Solar Tracker project represents the fusion of innovation, sustainability, and teamwork in modern engineering design. Developed by The Martians team under ENGR-102B and sponsored by Salt River Project, the system was engineered to maximize photovoltaic efficiency by automatically aligning a solar panel perpendicular to sunlight.

The prototype utilizes an Arduino-based microcontroller, four photoresistors for light detection, and two servo motors for dual-axis motion control. Through iterative prototyping, calibration of deadband, and real-time sensor feedback, the team achieved optimized system response and improved tracking accuracy.

Experimental data demonstrated a measurable increase in power generation compared to a fixed panel, validating the tracker's functional success. Beyond its technical accomplishments, the project promoted interdisciplinary collaboration, problem solving, and leadership — core principles of engineering education that transform ideas into working systems capable of advancing renewable energy technologies.

PhotovoltaicClosed-loopArduinoEmbedded3D-printedRenewableInterdisciplinary
§ 02 / PERFORMANCE

Interactive Dashboard / Power Predictor

Input

// Set irradiance · fit from measured data

Light Intensity E864.7W/m²
Panel Area A0.01165m²
137 × 85 mm · default panel
I = 4.0e-6 · E − 2.9e-5  [A]
V = 7.9e-3 · E − 1.414  [V]
P = V × I              [W]
η = (V·I)/(E·A) × 100 [%]

Predicted Output

// Real-time computation from curve-fit

Voltage
5.42V
Current
3.43mA
Power
18.61mW
Real Power (E·A)
10.07W
Efficiency η
0.185%
Important caveat. The 6.24 V and 2.9 mA readings were taken separately — voltage on the voltmeter setting (effectively Voc, open-circuit) and current on the ammeter setting (effectively Isc, short-circuit). The product Voc × Isc = 18 mW is not the deliverable power at the maximum power point; it is an upper bound. Real-world PMPP for a low-cost 3-cell module is typically 0.55–0.65 × Voc·Isc (fill factor), placing actual η closer to 0.10–0.12%. A proper I–V curve trace with a variable load (1 kΩ potentiometer sweep) was not performed during the meet; this is a known gap in the characterization.

Current vs. Light Intensity

// n=5 measurements · linear fit
I = 4.0×10⁻⁶·E − 2.9×10⁻⁵R² = 0.9958

Voltage vs. Light Intensity

// PIECEWISE LINEAR FIT · VALID 810–1015 W/m² ONLY · R²=0.51 INDICATES SATURATION, NOT LINEARITY
V = 7.9×10⁻³·E − 1.414R² = 0.5134
§ 03 / SYSTEM

Closed-loop architecture.

Four LDRs form a 2×2 cross. Opposite-pair differentials drive two servos through a deadband comparator. The control law is PWM-driven, runs at a 60 ms cadence, and constrains both axes to 0–180° to prevent cable tear-out.

Subsystem inventory.

  • 01
    LDR Array (cross)
    Four matched photoresistors mounted in a 2×2 cross inside a shrouded cannon to enforce directional sensitivity.
    GL5528 · 10–20 kΩ @ 10 lx
  • 02
    Voltage Divider
    10 kΩ reference resistor per LDR converts conductance to a readable analog voltage. Output increases monotonically with irradiance.
    V = Vcc · R / (R + R_LDR)
  • 03
    Arduino UNO R3
    10-bit ADC samples all four channels, computes left/right and top/bottom pair sums, applies a deadband comparator, and issues PWM to two servos.
    ATmega328P · 16 MHz · 1024 step ADC
  • 04
    Dual Servos (X, Y)
    SG90-class servos on pins 9 & 10. 3° step per loop iteration; constrained 0–180° to protect the harness.
    PWM 50 Hz · 60 ms loop cadence
  • 05
    3D-Printed Chassis
    Four modular parts: conical base, middle support, panel arm, LDR mount. Parametric dimensions; screw+adhesive bonded.
    PLA · 0.2 mm layer · 4-part assembly
  • 06
    Power Distribution
    USB 5 V supplies both logic and servo rails via the UNO's onboard regulator. Servos draw ~300–700 mA under stall; a decoupling cap on the servo rail is recommended to prevent brown-out. Future revisions should split the servo rail onto a separate 5 V supply.
    5 V USB · shared rail (known limitation)
§ 04 / FIRMWARE

Arduino control logic.

Ninety-three lines of embedded C. Four analog reads, two comparisons, two PWM writes, sixty millisecond loop. Click any line range to reveal its purpose. Authored by Alexander Burchuladze · 13 Oct 2025.

01 · LINES 52–55
Sense

Four analogRead() calls sample the photoresistors LDR_TL, LDR_TR, LDR_BR, LDR_BL through the ATmega328P's 10-bit ADC. Each returns an integer 0–1023 proportional to the light falling on that corner.

02 · LINES 58–61
Aggregate

Four pair-sums are computed: leftSum = tl + bl, rightSum = tr + br, topSum = tl + tr, bottomSum = bl + br. Summing corners into sides averages per-sensor noise and produces two clean orthogonal error signals — one for azimuth, one for elevation.

03 · LINES 68–79
Decide

Two symmetric comparators. The azimuth branch compares leftSum vs rightSum; elevation compares topSum vs bottomSum. Only when the difference exceeds deadband does the corresponding position update — by ±stepSize in the corrective direction. Inside the deadband, the panel holds still.

04 · LINES 82–86
Actuate

constrain(posX, 0, 180) and constrain(posY, 0, 180) clamp the commanded angles to protect the cable harness. servoX.write(posX) and servoY.write(posY) then push the targets to the Servo library, which generates 50 Hz PWM pulses on pins 9 and 10.

That full sense→aggregate→decide→actuate loop completes in about 60 ms (throttled by delay(loopDelay) on line 93), giving a control cadence of roughly 16.7 Hz. The sun moves at ~0.004°/s; the servos can slew at 50°/s. That four-order-of-magnitude margin is why a comparator-plus-deadband controller with no clock, no model of the sky, and no persistent state is sufficient: the plant is slow, the sensor is fast, and the error signal is physically forced toward zero whenever the panel is off-axis. The 93 lines below are the complete implementation.

LINES 29 — 31 · TUNING

Calibration parameters.

Three integers tune the entire control behavior. deadband=50 sets the minimum differential required between the two opposite pair-sums (not per-sensor values). Since leftSum = tl + bl ranges 0–2046, a deadband of 50 is ~2.4% of full-scale — effectively a ~25 LSB per-sensor threshold, well above LDR noise. stepSize sets the angular increment per loop iteration. loopDelay throttles the 60 ms update cadence.
§ 05 / SIMULATION

Live control simulation.

Watch the virtual sun sweep the sky. Four virtual LDRs compute illumination from projected angle, feed the deadband comparator, and command the servo to minimize azimuth error. Toggle the deadband to see hunting behavior.

SIM · AZIMUTH PLANE
SUN 0°
PANEL 90°
ERROR 0°
Δ L-R 0

Input

// Drive the sun · observe servo response

Sun Azimuth90°
Deadband50Δ
Step Size3°/loop
LDR LEFT
512
LDR RIGHT
512
if (leftSum > rightSum + deadband) posX -= stepSize;
else if (rightSum > leftSum + deadband) posX += stepSize;
§ 06 / METHOD

Data collection protocol.

A light-intensity meter and AstroAI AM33D multimeter were used in parallel. Irradiance (W/m²) and panel V/I were recorded simultaneously across the day. Power was computed as P = IV and efficiency as η = P/(E·A).

STEP 01

Record irradiance

Aim the light-intensity meter at the sun. Wait for reading to stabilize. Record E in W/m².

STEP 02

Measure voltage

Crocodile clamps across panel terminals. Black→COM, Red→VΩmA. Dial to 20V range.

STEP 03

Measure current

Power off meter. Move red probe to 10A port. Re-enable in 200 mA range. Record I.

Efficiency Calculator / Interactive

Measured inputs

// Plug in your own readings

Voltage V6.24V
Current I2.9mA
Irradiance E864W/m²
Panel area A0.01165m²

η output

// Live computation

EFFICIENCY
0.18 %
P_model = 18.10 mW / P_real = 10.07 W
Note: V×I here is Voc·Isc — an upper bound, not the true MPP. Actual η is typically ~0.55–0.65× this value (fill factor).
§ 07 / FIELD TEST

Solar Track Meet · 21 Oct 2025.

Two SOLRMAP / UA OASIS snapshots bracket the testing window. Irradiance nearly tripled between 12:30 and 13:00 MST as the morning's high cirrus cleared. Full telemetry below: global, direct, and diffuse horizontal irradiance; temperature; humidity; barometric pressure; wind; dew point; and azimuth/zenith alignment.

Observation. Between 12:30 and 13:00 MST, global horizontal irradiance climbed from 337 to 865 W/m² — a 2.56× increase — while the tracker's azimuth migrated from 187° to 198°. Both voltage and current scaled approximately linearly with irradiance. The alignment was validated against the OASIS-reported zenith/azimuth; the tracker held perpendicular to the sun across the window.
§ 08 / ITERATION

From breadboard to final render.

An analog-case methodology — acquire a reference implementation, disassemble, document, then iterate. Four stages: research, concept sketching, parametric CAD, and final assembly. Each stage surfaced specific failure modes resolved before the next pass.

§ 08.1 · METHODOLOGY

Analog-case research.

To begin designing the tracker, Ray acquired a commercially-available solar tracker from Amazon as a research analog — a physical reference model to disassemble, document, and improve upon.

This approach mirrors the Sonoran Desert analog methodology Ray applies at UA's HiRISE Operations Center, where Arizona's arid topography serves as a terrestrial analog for studying Martian surface features in imagery returned by NASA's Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter (MRO). Study an accessible system first; iterate toward the novel.

§ 08.2 · Evolution timeline

01 · RESEARCH

Analog case

KEYESTUDIO DIY solar tracking kit (Amazon-sourced) studied as a reference — LCD, BH1750 light sensor, DHT11 temperature/humidity module, cross-mounted LDRs. Disassembled to analyze mount geometry, servo placement, and cable routing before committing to our own parametric CAD.

02 · CONCEPT

Hand sketch

Conical base with hollow interior for cable management. LDR cannon mounted atop the panel arm.

03 · CAD

Parametric CAD

Four parts: base (85 mm), middle support, arm, LDR mount. Dimensions in mm; tolerances for servo clearance.

04 · FINAL

Final prototype

3D-printed PLA, screw+adhesive bonded. 100° software rotation limit added to protect harness.

§ 08.3 · Assembly troubleshooting log

FAULT 01

Servo blade clearance

Blade didn't fit the designated mount — interference on press-fit.
FIX: Shaved material from the blade interface using a metal file until clearance was achieved.
FAULT 02

Servo housing fit

Servo bodies couldn't seat in their printed pockets.
FIX: Enlarged the pocket walls with a metal file, then secured with screws + super glue to eliminate play.
FAULT 03

Reversed polarity

First power-up: panel rotated away from the light source. LDR wiring was flipped.
FIX: Reversed the analog-pin mapping so the control sign matched the physical geometry.
FAULT 04

Cable tear-out

At full 180° travel the arm snagged the harness and ripped connectors loose.
FIX: Added a 100° soft stop in firmware via constrain() to cap azimuth travel.

§ 08.4 · Parametric CAD dimensions (Paige Ann Brames)

Panel
137 × 85 mm
MCU
75 × 55 mm
Breadboard
85 × 55 mm

Original design credit: Simple Circuits.

The core control concept — four LDRs in cross configuration, opposite-pair differentials driving pan/tilt servos — originates with this open-source tutorial. The Martians' build adapts and extends it with a custom 3D-printed chassis, deadband tuning, and field validation at OASIS.

Simple, M. (2024, Dec 1). Build a Dual-Axis Solar Tracking System Using Arduino. Simple Circuits.

§ 10 / TEAM

The Martians.

Three students, interdisciplinary — Electrical & Computer Engineering, Mechanical Engineering, Biomedical Engineering — under the supervision of Dr. Armin Sorooshian. The name represents exploration, innovation, and adaptability — traits shared with the Mars missions that rely on renewable energy and autonomous systems.

Team Lead · Programming · Research

Raymundo Olguin

Electrical & Computer Engineering
HiRISE Research Engineering Technician
Led system integration, embedded programming, and mechanical assembly. Drove control-logic calibration (deadband, stepSize, loopDelay), authored the HiRISE-inspired DTM cover art, and coordinated documentation.
ArduinoControlSystemsHiRISE
Programming Lead · Research Lead

Alexander Burchuladze

Mechanical Engineering
Developed and optimized the dual-axis control firmware. Conducted data analysis to validate system accuracy. Collaborated on wiring and servo integration; authored the Tinkercad circuit schematic.
FirmwareCircuitData
Structural Design · CAD Lead

Paige Ann Brames

Biomedical Engineering
Oversaw the physical configuration — CAD documentation, 3D modeling, 3D-printing integration, and structural optimization. Parametric dimensioning ensured modular, manufacturable parts.
CAD3D PrintMechanical
Faculty Advisor

Dr. Armin Sorooshian

PhD Chemical Engineering · Caltech
Provided technical oversight and project validation to ensure engineering accuracy and performance optimization. Conducted the technical review that recognized the project as top of course.
AdvisoryValidation
What went well

Team collaboration and on-meet performance.

The tracker aligned accurately and functioned as designed on the day of the Solar Track Meet. Each team member brought complementary expertise — circuits and firmware from Ray and Alexander, mechanical and CAD from Paige — and the interdisciplinary handoffs stayed clean throughout the build.

What to improve

Time management and documentation cadence.

Parts of the report and late-stage testing were deferred to the final days. A better pass would include:

  • Weekly team meetings with agenda & action items
  • Writing the report in parallel with build, not after
  • A rehearsal session before the final presentation with divided sections and a shared script
